{"step_by_step_thinking": "The patient is an 8-year-old girl with a history of sore throat two weeks prior, which resolved without treatment. She now presents with hematuria, 1+ pitting edema, 3+ proteinuria, and an elevated antistreptolysin O titer. These findings are classic for post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN).

PSGN is a type III hypersensitivity reaction. In this mechanism, antibodies (specifically anti-streptolysin O) produced in response to a streptococcal infection bind to streptococcal antigens that have been deposited in the glomeruli. This leads to the formation of antigen-antibody complexes. These complexes activate the complement system, leading to inflammation, damage to the glomerular basement membrane, and the clinical manifestations of hematuria, proteinuria, edema, and hypertension.

Option A (Antigen-specific IgE binding to mast cells) describes type I hypersensitivity, which is not relevant here.
Option B (Tissue-specific antibodies) is more characteristic of autoimmune diseases like systemic lupus erythematosus, not post-infectious glomerulonephritis.
Option D (Presensitized CD8+ cytotoxic T-cells) is involved in type IV hypersensitivity, which is cell-mediated and not the mechanism in PSGN.

Therefore, the formation of antigen-antibody complexes is the key mechanism in this case.", "answer_choice": "C"}
